Infosys–Intel Tie-Up to Accelerate Enterprise AI Adoption

Infosys and Intel announced a strategic partnership to help enterprises scale AI from pilot projects to full production deployments.

The collaboration will combine Intel’s high-performance computing platforms with Infosys Topaz and AI services.

Solutions will be optimized for Intel Xeon processors, Intel Gaudi AI accelerators, and Intel AI PCs.

The partnership aims to create open standards across the edge-to-cloud AI ecosystem.

Focus areas include model management, data integration, performance monitoring, and built-in security features.

The companies will jointly design, develop, and benchmark enterprise AI workloads tailored to business needs.

AI solutions will support IT operations, developer productivity, and automation-led workflows.

The platform will also enable AI agents that can access enterprise data and coordinate tasks within governance frameworks.

SAR TELEVENTURE LIMITED: Announces Acquisition of Grand Foundry Limited

• 🤝 Acquisition Update: SAR Televenture has entered into a Share Purchase Agreement to acquire 70.17% stake in Grand Foundry Limited from existing promoters at ₹1.50 per share. 

• 📊 Open Offer Plan: The company will also acquire up to 26% additional stake from public shareholders at ₹2.50 per share through an open offer as per SEBI regulations. 

• 🏭 Strategic Diversification: Grand Foundry operates in iron, steel and telecom related segments, enabling SAR Televenture to diversify and consolidate its business portfolio. 

• 📈 Control Acquisition: Post completion and full open offer acceptance, SAR Televenture could hold up to 96.17% ownership, gaining effective control of the target company. 

• ⏳ Timeline: The acquisition process is expected to complete within six months of the public announcement. 

🟢 Impact: Positive
Strategic acquisition provides business diversification and potential restructuring opportunity, strengthening SAR Televenture’s long term growth strategy.

• 📊 Financial Impact: Management stated the order is not expected to materially impact financials or operations.

🟡 Impact: Neutral to Slight Negative

• 📊 Base Case Forecast: Brent crude expected to average ~$76/barrel in Q2 if supply stabilises.

🔴 Impact: Negative for Global Markets

Supreme Power Equipment Limited Begins Operations at New Facility; Capacity Expands to ~9,000 MVA

Supreme Power Equipment Limited has commenced commercial production at its new high-capacity transformer manufacturing facility located at Kannur Village, Thiruvallur District, near Chennai, Tamil Nadu

Key highlights of the new facility:
* Designed to manufacture power transformers up to 200 MVA and 220 kV
* Annual production capacity of 6,000–6,500 MVA
* Total installed manufacturing capacity now ~9,000 MVA per annum

Earlier, the company operated with an installed capacity of 2,500 MVA for transformers up to 110 kV, with capacity utilisation ranging between 70%–80%. The new facility significantly enhances manufacturing bandwidth by enabling production of higher voltage and larger MVA-rated transformers, allowing participation in more complex and large-scale power infrastructure projects

The expansion involves an investment of approximately ₹100 Cr and above towards infrastructure, advanced manufacturing capabilities and supporting facilities. The project has been funded through a balanced mix of IPO proceeds, internal accruals, debt and proceeds from a preferential issue, ensuring a diversified and prudent financing structure

The capacity augmentation is aligned with rising demand in the power transmission and distribution sector and is expected to enable execution of larger MVA contracts, strengthen medium-term revenue visibility and support sustained growth across utilities, industrial and infrastructure segments

⛽️ What $140 Oil Actually Means for India

If crude spikes to $140/barrel, the impact on India goes far beyond fuel prices.

1️⃣ Import Bill Surge
• India’s oil import bill could jump to ~$245 billion
• Massive dollar outflow puts pressure on forex reserves

2️⃣ Trade Deficit Widens
• Every $10 rise in oil → ~0.5% higher trade deficit
• At $140 oil, deficit could move toward 3–4%+

3️⃣ Rupee Pressure
• Higher oil → larger deficit → rupee depreciation
• Weaker rupee makes oil even costlier in INR

4️⃣ Inflation & EMIs Rise
• A $60+ oil spike can lift inflation 2–3%
• RBI may raise interest rates, pushing up loan EMIs

5️⃣ Limited Fiscal Cushion
• Fiscal deficit already around 4.5–5%
• Limited scope for fuel tax cuts or subsidies

6️⃣ Hormuz Risk
• ~50% of India’s crude flows through Strait of Hormuz
• Any disruption could mean price spike + supply shock

7️⃣ Larger Economy, Larger Impact
• Oil share of GDP lower than before
• But import volumes have doubled, increasing absolute risk

8️⃣ Sector Impact
• Airlines, transport, paints, chemicals see margin pressure
• Lower profits → less tax revenue & potential job cuts

9️⃣ Capital Flow Risk
• Oil shock can trigger FII outflows
• Leads to weaker rupee & higher borrowing costs

🔟 Renewables Are Long-Term
• Solar & EVs help structurally
• But cannot offset a sudden oil shock

📌 Bottom Line:
India is stronger today, but oil remains a key macro vulnerability.
A sustained $140 crude shock would hit inflation, currency, growth, and household budgets.

Singtel to Cut Stake as Airtel Simplifies Promoter Structure

Bharti Airtel plans to consolidate promoter holdings under Bharti Telecom (BTL) as part of a restructuring roadmap.

Bharti Telecom currently holds ~41% stake in Airtel, making it the main promoter vehicle.

About 7% stake held by Singtel and ~1% held by the Mittal family outside BTL will be gradually reduced via market transactions over the next 3–4 years.

After restructuring, Bharti Telecom will remain the sole direct promoter entity with 41% stake in Airtel.

Singtel and the Mittal family will pare their residual direct holdings to zero, increasing the public float.

The plan is part of a broader promoter restructuring first outlined in August 2022 to simplify and equalise holdings.

Overall promoter shareholding in Airtel has already declined from 55.9% (June 2022) to about 48.9% currently.

India holds about 100 million barrels of commercial 
crude oil stocks - in storage tanks, underground strategic 
reserves and on ships voyaging towards the country - which could cover roughly 40-45 days of its requirement if flows through the Strait of Hormuz are disrupted, according to Kpler.
India imports about 88 per cent of the crude oil it needs the raw material for fuels such as petrol and diesel - with more than 50 per cent supplied by Middle Eastern countries and transiting the narrow Strait of Hormuz, flows from which have been disrupted amid the Iran crisis.
If Middle Eastern crude supply were to halt completely for 
a temporary period, the immediate impact would be logistical and price-driven, with supply risks intensifying if movement through the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted for longer, said Sumit Ritolia, Lead Research Analyst, Refining & Modeling at Kpler.
A closure of Strait of Hormuz would at first impact prompt 
cargo liftings. "However, refiners typically maintain commercial inventories, and cargoes already on water would continue to arrive, providing some short-term cushioning to the system," he said adding in the event of a prolonged disruption, medium-term pressures would build through higher import costs, freight exposure and the need to reroute supplies over longer distances.
"The country maintains strategic petroleum reserves 
alongside commercial inventories held by refiners and oil 
marketing companies. These buffers are intended to manage temporary supply shocks rather than sustained outages," he said. 
"Based on Kpler inventory data, commercial crude stocks are 
around 100 million barrels, including volumes in the strategic 
petroleum reserve (SPR) facilities at Mangalore, Padur and 
Visakhapatnam."
With imports via the Strait of Hormuz averaging roughly 
2.5 million barrels per day - about half of India's just over 5 
million bpd total crude imports - these combined reserves could theoretically cover around 40-45 days of imports in a crude disruption scenario, he said.
Additional refined product inventories would extend 
effective coverage further. However, the immediate impact will be on prices. Brent, global benchmark, crossed USD 80 per barrel, roughly 10 per cent more since the Iran crisis. For India, higher prices means higher import bill.
India spent USD 137 billion on crude oil imports in fiscal 
year ended March 31, 2025. During April 2025 to January 2026 - first ten months of current fiscal - it spent USD 100.4 billion on import of 206.3 million tonnes of crude oil.
The United States and Israel launched military strikes on 
targets in Iran over the weekend. Tehran retaliated with 
missiles and drones aimed at Israel and countries hosting US forces, including the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait, 
Bahrain, Iraq, Jordan and Saudi Arabia. Media reports suggest the conflict has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a key conduit for global energy flows. Roughly one-third of the world's seaborne crude oil exports and about 20 per cent of liquefied natural gas shipments transit the narrow waterway.
India, the world's third largest oil importer, imports 
roughly half of its crude needs through the narrow Strait. Its 
mainstay liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplier in Qatar also uses the strait to ship the fuel to India. In case of closure, India can tap suppliers in West Africa, Latin Amercia and the US to make up for the shortfall from the Middle-East. India could also tap Russian oil to make up for the deficit.
India had agreed to wind down purchases of Russian oil as 
part of a trade deal with the US - a deal which now sits in 
limbo after the US Supreme Court struck down US President Donald Trump's country-based tariffs. "Russian cargoes currently floating in the Arabian Sea and wider Asian region without firm buyers could also be absorbed relatively quickly if required," Ritolia said.

reports.text:
If the Strait of Hormuz - the narrow 33-kilometre passage 
connecting the Persian Gulf to the Arabian Sea - were disrupted or shipping were forced onto longer routes, India's crude import bill would rise.
Even without a full blockade, higher freight, war-risk 
insurance and geopolitical premiums would lift landed costs.
"In a more severe scenario, policy intervention would 
likely become a key stabilising tool. The government could 
prioritise domestic energy security by asking refiners to 
moderate or temporarily curb refined product exports to ensure adequate domestic supply," he said.
Given that India is among the world's largest exporters of 
refined products, particularly diesel and jet, redirecting 
export volumes to the domestic market would provide an 
additional buffer.
India exported 23.7 million tonnes (474,000 bpd) of 
petroleum products or 10 per cent of the country's fuel 
consumption, in 2024-25. During April-January, exports stood at 53.3 million tones.
"If the Hormuz situation were to deteriorate meaningfully, 
safeguarding domestic fuel availability and price stability 
would likely take precedence over export optimisation, 
reinforcing internal supply resilience even at the cost of lower export revenues," he said.
The worst-case scenario would involve a prolonged and 
severe disruption to Hormuz flows combined with sustained 
geopolitical escalation. In that case, crude prices would spike sharply, freight markets would tighten, and refiners could eventually be forced to trim runs if replacement barrels are delayed.
"However, such a scenario would have major global economic consequences, making it a low-probability but high-impact risk," he said adding the near-term risk is primarily price volatility and higher import costs rather than immediate physical shortage, for now.
India has diversification options and inventory buffers, 
but sustained disruption would materially increase the import bill and create macroeconomic pressures.

Midcap IT Double Down on AI-Led Mega Deals to Take on Tier-1 Players  

Strategic Pivot to AI-Led Large Deals  
- Mid-tier IT firms are aggressively shifting to AI-first, large multi-year deals to stay relevant amid GenAI disruption and changing client expectations.  
- Narrative is moving from experimentation to AI-native core transformation, with clients demanding clear, outcome-linked value.  

Big Wins for Zensar, LTIMindtree, Coforge  
- Zensar Technologies has bagged a $210 million, 5.5-year deal with a large financial services player, centred on AI-native enterprise transformation and automation.  
- LTIMindtree has secured a $100 million European healthcare contract, along with a large AI-driven tax analytics modernisation deal in India, underscoring its data and AI depth.  
- Coforge has won a $158 million, five-year UK deal and forged a strategic partnership with US-based VHC Health to drive AI-led infrastructure, cloud and digital workplace transformation. 

From Pilots to Full-Scale AI Deployment  
- Clients are moving beyond PoCs and pilots to enterprise-wide AI rollouts, embedding AI into core systems and operations.  
- Boardroom focus is on governance, resilience, operational efficiency and measurable ROI, making AI-linked KPIs central to contract structures.  

Europe Emerges as a Hotspot  
- Deal sizes and win frequency are rising, particularly in Europe, where enterprises are favouring phased modernisation with modular AI capabilities.  
- Midcaps are leveraging speed, flexibility and domain focus to win against larger incumbents in competitive RFPs.  

Competitive Dynamics with Tier-1 IT  
- The shift to AI-embedded, longer-tenure contracts is blurring the line between midcaps and large-cap IT on deal profile.  
- Differentiation is increasingly driven by AI platforms, execution speed and ability to co-innovate rather than just scale and legacy relationships.  

Revenue Visibility and Growth Construct  
- Multi-year, AI-centric deals are becoming the core engine for revenue visibility, supporting better growth predictability for midcap IT.  
- As AI becomes non-discretionary spend, these contracts can lift annuity revenues, expand wallet share and support margin resilience over the cycle.  

Key Takeaway  
AI-led mega deals are turning midcap IT into serious contenders to Tier-1 firms, with long-tenure, outcome-linked contracts poised to structurally rerate growth and visibility for the segment.

Semicon 2.0 to Focus on Chip Design and Deep-Tech Innovation  

Strategic Shift  
- Union IT Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw unveils Semicon Mission 2.0 with focus on design ecosystem and deep-tech startups.  
- Moves beyond manufacturing-led Semicon 1.0 to prioritize chip design, innovation, and equipment manufacturing.  

Core Objective  
- Build a robust semiconductor design ecosystem enabling Indian startups to develop advanced chips and IPs.  
- Government aims to foster companies that could become India’s Qualcomm, Broadcom, or Nvidia.  

Talent & Capability Push  
- India faces a 2 million talent gap in the semiconductor industry—seen as a major opportunity for engineers and students.  
- Initiative to invest in design infrastructure, training, and R&D to close this skills gap.  

Progress So Far  
- 10 semiconductor plants approved under the national mission.  
- One plant already commercial, and another nearing production start.  

Next Steps  
- Align chip design output with domestic fabs for Made-in-India semiconductor production.  
- Scale up equipment manufacturing and design infrastructure to complete the semiconductor value chain.  

Key Takeaway:  
Semicon 2.0 marks India’s pivot from fabs to innovation—aiming to transform the country into a global semiconductor design powerhouse.

Nvidia Teams Up with Telcos to Build AI-Native 6G Networks  

Strategic Alliance  
- Nvidia partners with Nokia, SoftBank Group, and T-Mobile US to co-develop AI-native 6G infrastructure.  
- Target: reinvent how future mobile networks handle AI-driven workloads and intelligent devices.  

Why It Matters  
- Current 5G systems optimized for voice and data—not designed for the coming wave of AI traffic.  
- 6G networks will integrate AI at the core, supporting robots, autonomous systems, and real-time analytics.  

Technology Vision  
- Future architecture to feature AI-powered software, open systems, and programmable radios.  
- Shifts from hardware-heavy designs to cloud-like networks that can self-manage, scale, and adapt rapidly.  

Business Angle  
- Positions Nvidia to expand its AI chips and software ecosystem into telecom infrastructure.  
- Represents a new growth frontier, merging data center intelligence with wireless technology.  

Industry Implication  
- Early collaboration aims to define global 6G standards capable of supporting large-scale AI deployment.  
- Signals the start of convergence between AI computing and next-gen connectivity.  

Key Takeaway:  
Nvidia is moving beyond data centers—embedding AI directly into future telecom networks to power the intelligent, connected economy of the 6G era.

**Gravita India (CMP: 1,594): Strategic Expansions Fuel Multi-Year Compounder Story**

**Strategic Moves**  
- Acquired Rashtriya Metals Industries (RMIL) for Rs 565 crore – entry into copper recycling/alloys; total copper capacity now 340 ktpa, +45 ktpa commissioning this quarter, fully onstream by Q2FY27.  
- Mundra lead recycling expanded to 145,100 TPA (Rs 49 crore capex via internal accruals).  
- 6,000 TPA Li-ion battery recycling plant commissioned at Mundra (Rs 14 crore capex) – taps EV/clean energy circular economy theme.

**Q3FY26 Performance**  
- Revenue Rs 1,017 crore (+2.1% YoY); EBITDA Rs 111 crore (+25.2% YoY, OPM 11.0% up 200 bps); PAT Rs 98 crore (+25.3% YoY), EPS Rs 13.4.  
- Lead core driver (85-90% volumes/revenue): Q3 lead revenue +9% YoY to Rs 915 crore; EBITDA/kg Rs 23 (+21% YoY); guides 18-20% lead growth, EBITDA/kg Rs 19-20 stable.  
- Aluminium weak (-33% revenue YoY, -43% volumes); plastics revenue -39% YoY but EBITDA/kg improves to Rs 10.

**Growth Outlook**  
- Q4FY26 volume growth guided at 8-9%; long-term revenue/APAT CAGRs ~18%/~26% with RoCE ~18%.  
- Vision 2029: 25%+ volume CAGR, 35%+ profitability CAGR FY25-FY29; 50% revenue from value-added products by FY29.  
- Tailwinds from rising scrap availability, BMWR/EPR/RCM policies, EV/clean energy demand favoring Mundra/new lines.

**Valuation & View**  
- Trades at 33x/27x FY27E/FY28E EPS; FY28E EPS Rs 71.3, RoCE ~18%, RoNW 17-18%.  
- Mirae Asset Sharekhan: Buy, TP Rs 2,000 (28x FY28E EPS, ~25% upside).

**Key Risks**  
- Regulatory issues around scrap procurement and policy changes impacting sourcing.  
- Global aluminium price volatility tightening scrap availability/processing volumes.

**Key Takeaway**: Gravita's copper/Li-ion push atop lead strength sets up 25%+ volume CAGR to FY29, but execution amid aluminium volatility and regulatory risks will determine re-rating potential.

**Why Businesses Fail: 2 Simple Financial Rules**

**Rule 1: ROCE > Cost of Capital**  
Money costs 12-15% (equity/debt).  
**ROCE 10% vs CoC 14% = 4% value destruction annually.**  
Sustainable wealth only when ROCE consistently exceeds CoC.  

**Rule 2: Cash In Before Cash Out**  
Profits ≠ cash.  
**Short-term debt for long-term assets creates repayment mismatches.**  
OCF must cover debt servicing needs for stability.  

**Simple Test**  
- ROCE > CoC → **Value creation**  
- OCF ≥ Debt needs → **Financial stability**  

**Key Takeaway:**  
90% failures trace to these gaps. Master both rules = survive cycles, compound wealth.

**Sakar Healthcare Ltd – Oncology-Led Re‑rating Story (Educational)**  

**Oncology Growth Engine**  
- Oncology revenues are projected to jump from ~₹33 Cr in FY25 to ~₹350 Cr by FY28, implying ~118% CAGR.  
- This materially shifts the mix towards higher-margin, specialty products and can drive a step-up in overall profitability.  

**Integrated Oncology Platform**  
- Vertically integrated model with in-house API and formulation in oncology supports better margins, supply security, and stronger pricing power.  
- This integration also improves control over quality and regulatory compliance – crucial for global oncology play.  

**Regulatory & Market Expansion**  
- Target of 250–300 Marketing Authorisations (MAs) by FY27 across 48 countries builds a diversified global franchise.  
- Deeper penetration in EU plus emerging markets reduces single-region risk and expands the addressable market.  

**Partnership Upside (Accord Healthcare)**  
- Supply agreement with Accord Healthcare for EU oncology portfolio carries medium-term revenue potential of >₹450 Cr.  
- Successful scale-up under this partnership can accelerate utilisation ramp-up and validate Sakar as a credible global oncology supplier.  

**Capacity Operating Leverage**  
- Bavla oncology plant has revenue potential of up to ~₹1,000 Cr at ~80% utilisation with no major additional capex.  
- As volumes ramp, fixed-cost absorption should boost margins, RoCE and operating cash flows.  

**Base Business Cushion**  
- Non-oncology CMO/CDMO business provides a stable base, smoothing earnings while oncology scales up.  
- This offers downside support if ramp-up in oncology is slower than expected.  

**Valuation Context**  
- At ~15x FY28E earnings, the stock is baking in strong growth but still reasonable versus the scale of oncology option value, provided execution/regulatory milestones are met.  

**Key Takeaway:**  
Sakar Healthcare is pivoting from a stable CMO/CDMO base to a high-growth, integrated global oncology player, with capacity already in place and multiple triggers (MAs, Accord partnership, utilisation) that can drive earnings and potential re-rating over FY25–28, subject to execution and regulatory delivery.
